prevalence.source: Orphanet; inborn errors of metabolism clinical registry syntheses
prevalence.trend: stable; earlier recognition improving with metabolic testing access

incidence.source: International IEM incidence syntheses; tertiary center reports

burden.dalyPerCapita: very low (rare), but high per-patient burden (metabolic crises, cardiomyopathy, neurodisability)
burden.mortalityRate: Low overall; higher in severe neonatal presentations without rapid treatment
burden.source: Orphanet clinical burden summaries; IHME/GBD modeled context

riskFactors: protein load in infancy without metabolic management
vulnerablePopulations: patients in regions without metabolic reference centers
patients with recurrent infections or poor nutrition
epidemiology.diagnosticCriteria: MS/MS acylcarnitine profile with elevated C3, metabolic acidosis and hyperammonemia, urine organic acids (3-hydroxypropionate, methylcitrate), confirmatory enzymatic/genetic testing

publicHealthSystem.screeningCoverage: limited and heterogeneous; confirmatory testing mainly in reference laboratories
publicHealthSystem.controlRate: Moderate with early diagnosis and adherence to diet/medication; low where access is fragmented

regionalSpecificities.diagnosisCriteria: Case detection via neonatal/infant decompensation in emergency settings; referral for confirmatory MS/MS and genetics
regionalSpecificities.treatmentProtocol: Emergency decompensation protocol (caloric support, ammonia control), long-term protein restriction with medical formulas, carnitine, intermittent antibiotics for gut propionate (e.g., metronidazole), cardiology/neuro follow-up
regionalSpecificities.regionalChallenges: Late diagnosis, access gaps for specialized formulas and emergency protocols outside tertiary hospitals
regionalSpecificities.strongPoints: Tertiary pediatric centers increasingly familiar with IEM emergency management
